quizzes.title: Ch 9 Quiz
quizzes.description: Ch. 9 Quiz mapped from the completed OTS.5110.ONL-2026SP attempt for March 30 - April 5.

questions.question: The title of "Deuteronomy" is associated with "second law."
questions.explanation: The correct answer is True.

questions.question: The narrative setting of the book is on top of Mount Sinai.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is False.

questions.question: The narrative framework of the book is Moses' farewell address to the Israelites.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is True.

questions.question: Monotheism is the belief in the oneness and consistency of God.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is True.

questions.question: The Shema is in Exodus 19:4.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is False.

questions.question: Deuteronomy 28 describes the blessings and curses of the covenant.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is True.

questions.question: Deuteronomic theology can be described as a theology of ecclesiology.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is False.

questions.question: The suzerainty treaties were political covenants between unequal partners.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is True.

questions.question: Joshua has a positive view of the Israelites' obedience at the end of the book.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is False.

questions.question: The Deuteronomistic History consists of Genesis, Exodus, Leviticus, Numbers, and Deuteronomy.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is False.

quizzes.title: Ch 10 Quiz
quizzes.description: Ch. 10 Quiz mapped from the completed OTS.5110.ONL-2026SP attempt for April 6 - April 12.

questions.question: The "Historical Books" are important in part because of their historical content.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is True.

questions.question: These books describe Israel's history, but they are more than history or a mere record of historical facts.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is True.

questions.question: The books Isaiah, Ezekiel, Jeremiah, and The Twelve are known as the Former Prophets.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is False.

questions.question: Heilsgeschichte is a German term that denotes "salvation history."
questions.explanation: The correct answer is True.

questions.question: Judges is a happy book detailing Israel's single-minded devotion to God.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is False.

questions.question: All of the historical books are authored by the same person after whom the book is titled.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is False.

questions.question: The classic expression of the Deuteronomistic History was given by Martin Noth.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is True.

questions.question: The notion of a Deuteronomistic History is sometimes used to describe a single composition from Deuteronomy through Kings in the Hebrew order.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is True.

questions.question: Acknowledging the overarching philosophy of retribution theology in history brings a certain unity to the historical books.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is True.

questions.question: Chronicles, Ezra-Nehemiah, Ruth, and Esther are not in the same arranged order in the Hebrew order of the Bible as they are in the English order of the Bible.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is True.

quizzes.title: Ch 11-12 Quiz
quizzes.description: Ch. 11-12 Quiz mapped from the completed OTS.5110.ONL-2026SP attempt for April 13 - April 19.

questions.question: The outline for the book of Joshua was Israel Conquers the Land (1-12), Israel Divides the Land (13-21), and Israel Begins to Settle into the Land (22-24).
questions.explanation: The correct answer is True.

questions.question: Joshua 24:13 indicates that Joshua is the author when it says, "I, Joshua, write this greeting in my own hand."
questions.explanation: The correct answer is False.

questions.question: The mention of Jebusites in Jerusalem and Canaanites in Gezer suggest that the book was in its present form no later than Solomon's time.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is True.

questions.question: The Davidic covenant is a theme of the book of Joshua according to the textbook.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is False.

questions.question: The expression "in those days Israel had no king" suggests the author's perspective was from the later vantage point of the monarchy.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is True.

questions.question: The judges of the Bible act precisely as our contemporary judges act in America.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is False.

questions.question: The author of the book of Judges used a cycle of sin, war as judgment, repentance, and deliverance in order to introduce certain judges.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is True.

questions.question: Historical problems in the book of Judges include chronology and political structure.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is True.

questions.question: Scholars have concluded that an amphictyony describes exactly what was occurring in the days of the judges.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is False.

questions.question: I read all of I Samuel.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is True.

quizzes.title: Ch 13-14 Quiz
quizzes.description: Ch. 13-14 Quiz mapped from the completed OTS.5110.ONL-2026SP attempt for April 20 - April 26.

questions.question: Saul was successful as king because he obeyed the commands of God.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is False.

questions.question: The textbook lists David's rise to prominence as a theme in 1 Samuel.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is True.

questions.question: The textbook indicates that the sentence in 1 Sam 27:6, "Zigklag has belonged to the kings of Judah to this day" suggests some time had elapsed since the division of the kingdom.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is True.

questions.question: The book of 1 Samuel reports that Samuel wrote the book.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is False.

questions.question: God looked at David's family lineage in order to choose him.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is False.

questions.question: The textbook lists God's covenant with David as a theme in 2 Samuel.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is True.

questions.question: Jerusalem was Israel's spiritual center as well as its political center.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is True.

questions.question: David wanted to build God a house, but God told David he would build him a "house" of descendants.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is True.

questions.question: The word "covenant" is not used in 2 Samuel 7.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is True.

questions.question: God's "covenant" with David included a provision to include Saul's son in the dynasty.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is False.

quizzes.title: W10 Quiz 1
quizzes.description: Deuteronomy as Moses sermonic explanation of the law and Israel story

questions.question: How does the lecture primarily define Deuteronomy?

options.text: Moses sermon or commentary on Exodus through Numbers

questions.explanation: The lecture presents Deuteronomy as Moses sermonic exposition of prior Pentateuchal material for the generation about to enter Canaan.

questions.question: Where does the title Deuteronomy come from according to the lecture?

options.text: The Hebrew opening word of the book
The Septuagint idea of a second copy of the law
A rabbinic title from the exile

questions.explanation: The title reflects the Greek Septuagint rendering associated with Deuteronomy 17-18 and the idea of a second copy of the law.

questions.question: What caution does the lecture give about Pentateuchal legal codes?

options.text: They are only Roman civil law
They are historically situated and not a mechanism of salvation
They replace the promises to Abraham

questions.explanation: The laws are normative within their covenant purpose, but the lecture denies that Israel was saved by law keeping.

questions.question: Which later event is traditionally associated with Deuteronomy as the book of the law?

options.text: The fall of Samaria under Assyria
The building of the second temple by Herod

questions.explanation: The lecture notes the link between Deuteronomy and the book of the law found in Josiah reform.

questions.question: What point is made about ancient Near Eastern treaty forms?

options.text: They include features such as suzerain, witnesses, stipulations, and curses
They prove Deuteronomy has no covenant theology
They appear only in the New Testament

questions.explanation: The lecture compares Deuteronomy with Hittite and Assyrian treaty forms while warning that genre similarity alone does not settle meaning.

questions.question: Which items are discussed as possible indicators of later composition or framing?

options.text: Exile-resonant texts, kingship legislation, and prophetic expectation
Descriptions of the second temple sacrifices

questions.explanation: The lecture raises exile-related passages, Deuteronomy 17 kingship law, and Deuteronomy 18 and 34 prophetic themes as dating questions.

questions.question: Why does Deuteronomy 1:1-5 raise authorship or perspective questions?

options.text: It contains no reference to Moses
It is written entirely in Aramaic
Its third-person frame speaks from beyond the Jordan in Moab
It identifies Joshua as the author

questions.explanation: The third-person introduction and the phrase beyond the Jordan can sound like a narrator located outside Moses immediate perspective.

questions.question: What does Moses begin doing in Deuteronomy 1:5?

options.text: Explaining the law by retelling and interpreting Israel history

questions.explanation: The lecture emphasizes that Moses explains the law by narrating and interpreting the story, not merely by listing statutes.

questions.question: Why will Moses not enter the promised land?

options.text: He fails to treat the LORD as holy in Numbers 20
He is too young to lead

questions.explanation: The lecture links Moses exclusion from the land to Numbers 20:12, where he fails to trust and treat the LORD as holy.

questions.question: Which chapters are identified as a major legal core in Deuteronomy?

options.text: Deuteronomy 12-26, with legal material also in chapter 5

questions.explanation: The lecture identifies Deuteronomy 12-26 as the major legal section and also notes chapter 5.

quizzes.title: W10 Quiz 2
quizzes.description: Israel rebellion, heart circumcision, blessings and curses, and the Moab covenant

questions.question: Why does Deuteronomy say Israel receives the land?

options.text: Because Moses defeated every enemy alone
Because God is fulfilling his promise to the patriarchs

questions.explanation: Moses insists the land is not given because of Israel righteousness but because of God promise to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob.

questions.question: What episode functions as a paradigm of Israel rebellion in the lecture?
questions.explanation: The golden calf is treated as an archetypal example of Israel stiff-necked rebellion from the exodus onward.

questions.question: What does Deuteronomy 10:16 reveal about Israel basic problem?

options.text: It is an internal heart problem needing transformation
It is only a military problem
It is only a lack of land
It is a shortage of priests

questions.explanation: The command to circumcise the heart diagnoses an inward spiritual problem, not merely an external behavior problem.

questions.question: How does the lecture describe the end point of Deuteronomy 28 curses?

options.text: A return to an Egypt-like captivity
The replacement of Moses by Aaron

questions.explanation: The curses culminate in exile framed typologically as a return to Egypt-like captivity.

questions.question: What is significant about Deuteronomy 29:1?

options.text: It introduces a Moab covenant besides the Horeb/Sinai covenant

questions.explanation: The lecture reads Deuteronomy 29:1 as introducing a covenant in Moab besides the covenant at Horeb.

questions.question: What does Deuteronomy 29:4 say Israel still lacks?

options.text: A heart to know, eyes to see, and ears to hear

questions.explanation: The verse reinforces that Israel lacks the God-given internal perception needed for true obedience.

questions.question: How broad is the covenant invitation in Deuteronomy 29?

options.text: It includes leaders, children, wives, sojourners, and even those not present

questions.explanation: The lecture stresses the inclusive and forward-reaching scope of the Moab covenant.

questions.question: In the lecture, what does heart mean in the phrase circumcise your heart?

options.text: The inner person or whole being

questions.explanation: Heart is explained as the inner person or whole being, not merely emotion or the physical organ.

questions.question: What remains unresolved after Deuteronomy 11-26 gives laws and warnings?
questions.explanation: The legal code is given, but the lecture emphasizes that Israel internal heart problem has not yet been solved.

questions.question: Which two topics from Deuteronomy 17-18 are briefly noted in the legal material?

options.text: The king and the future prophet

questions.explanation: The lecture notes the law of the king in Deuteronomy 17 and the promise of a prophet in Deuteronomy 18.

quizzes.title: W10 Quiz 3
quizzes.description: Deuteronomy 30 as future restoration, heart circumcision, and the near word

questions.question: How does the lecture characterize Deuteronomy 30?

options.text: As a pivotal, future-oriented restoration chapter
As a list of priestly garments

questions.explanation: The lecture treats Deuteronomy 30 as a major future-looking chapter anticipating exile and restoration.

questions.question: What historical reality do the blessings and curses ultimately anticipate?

options.text: The end of all covenant promises
A permanent return to Eden before Joshua

questions.explanation: The lecture connects the blessings and curses to Israel later exile and dispersion among the nations.

questions.question: What is the central divine action promised in Deuteronomy 30:6?

options.text: God will cancel the Abrahamic promise
God will remove all written Scripture
God will circumcise Israel heart and the heart of their offspring
God will appoint Pharaoh over Israel

questions.explanation: God promises to circumcise the heart so Israel can love Yahweh fully and live.

questions.question: Which restoration elements are highlighted in Deuteronomy 30:3-5?

options.text: A ban on all future prophecy
Compassion, gathering, return to the land, and multiplication

questions.explanation: The lecture emphasizes restoration of fortunes, compassion, gathering from dispersion, return, and increase.

questions.question: How does Deuteronomy 30 clarify physical circumcision?

options.text: It is a sign pointing to God inward work
It has no relation to covenant

questions.explanation: Physical circumcision is interpreted as a sign that points beyond itself to inward divine transformation.

questions.question: What does Deuteronomy 30:11-14 say about the commandment or word?

options.text: It is inaccessible and hidden forever
It is near, in the mouth and heart, to do it
It is only in heaven and cannot be reached
It belongs only across the sea

questions.explanation: The word is not too difficult or distant; it is near, in the mouth and heart, to be obeyed.

questions.question: Which Exodus motifs are used to interpret the contrasts in Deuteronomy 30:11-14?

options.text: Plagues, Sinai distance, mountain ascent, and sea crossing

questions.explanation: The lecture connects wonderful, distant, heaven, and sea language with Exodus plagues, Sinai, Moses ascent, and sea-crossing imagery.

questions.question: How does Paul use Deuteronomy 30 in Romans 10 according to the lecture?

options.text: As proof that Gentiles cannot be saved
As a command to rebuild Jericho
As the preached word of faith near in mouth and heart

questions.explanation: The lecture reads Romans 10 as applying Deuteronomy 30 to the preached word of faith.

questions.question: Which later prophetic texts continue the restoration and heart-transformation theme?
questions.explanation: Jeremiah and Ezekiel are noted as carrying forward restoration and internal renewal expectations.

questions.question: What contrast does the lecture draw at the end of the discussion?

options.text: External written code versus God internal enabling work

questions.explanation: The lecture contrasts an external code that cannot permanently enable obedience with God internal work that empowers love and obedience.

quizzes.title: W10 Quiz 4
quizzes.description: Deuteronomy 31-34, covenant witnesses, idolatry, Gentile jealousy, and Moses future hope

questions.question: What tension opens this lecture?

options.text: Moses enters Canaan, yet Joshua stays behind
The law disappears, yet kings obey perfectly
God promises future heart circumcision, yet Israel immediate future is rebellion
Israel has no land promise, yet possesses land

questions.explanation: The lecture juxtaposes future heart renewal with the negative expectation that Israel will rebel and pursue idols.

questions.question: What does Deuteronomy 31 predict Israel will do?

options.text: Return voluntarily to Egypt before entering Canaan
Pursue strange gods, forsake the LORD, and break covenant

questions.explanation: Deuteronomy 31 explicitly predicts apostasy, covenant breaking, and pursuit of other gods.

questions.question: Which items function as covenant witnesses in Deuteronomy?

options.text: The Book of the Law, Moses words, the Song, and heaven and earth

questions.explanation: The lecture identifies multiple witnesses against Israel, including the written law, Moses words, the Song, and heaven and earth.

questions.question: How is Deuteronomy 31:29 described?

options.text: As a last-days statement about corruption and disaster from idolatry
As a census of the Levites

questions.explanation: The verse frames later corruption and evil in the last days as the result of provoking God by the work of their hands.

questions.question: What is the initial function of Deuteronomy 32:1-15?

options.text: It serves as a historical review of God care and Israel abandonment

questions.explanation: The Song begins with God guidance and blessing before indicting Israel for abandonment and idolatry.

questions.question: Which earlier idolatry texts are echoed in Deuteronomy 32:16-21?
questions.explanation: The lecture notes dense links to earlier Pentateuchal idolatry episodes and vocabulary.

questions.question: How does the lecture interpret not a people in Deuteronomy 32:21?

options.text: As God turning to Gentiles rather than merely military defeat
As a reference to priests alone
As a denial of Israel existence

questions.explanation: The lecture argues that jealousy language fits God placing love on another people, later read as Gentile inclusion.

questions.question: How does Paul use Deuteronomy 32:21?

options.text: To forbid all mission outside Judea
To explain Gentile inclusion that provokes Israel to jealousy
To prove Solomon fulfilled all promises

questions.explanation: Paul cites the passage in Romans to frame Gentile inclusion as part of God purpose toward Israel.

questions.question: What expectation remains at the end of Deuteronomy 34?
questions.explanation: Deuteronomy ends with Moses elevated and a forward-looking expectation for a prophet like him.

questions.question: What features of Deuteronomy 34 suggest a post-Mosaic perspective?

options.text: A direct signature from Moses after death
The unknown grave and the statement that no prophet like Moses had arisen

questions.explanation: The narrator knows Moses grave is unknown and looks back on the prophetic line, suggesting later framing.

quizzes.title: W11 Quiz 1
quizzes.description: The transition from Torah to Joshua and the recognition of the Hebrew canon

questions.question: What transition frames the Joshua and Canon lecture?

options.text: From Torah to Joshua and a discussion of canon

questions.explanation: The course has completed the Torah and turns to Joshua while opening a broader discussion of canon.

questions.question: What is the Hebrew Bible tripartite structure?
questions.explanation: The Hebrew Bible is organized as Torah, Neviim, and Ketuvim, or Torah, Prophets, and Writings.

questions.question: Where does Joshua stand in the Hebrew canonical order?
questions.explanation: Joshua begins the Former Prophets in the Hebrew arrangement.

questions.question: How does the lecture characterize canon formation?

options.text: As only a late council-created list
As a historical process of recognition

questions.explanation: The lecture stresses recognition and use of authoritative writings rather than merely a later council decision.

questions.question: Why are simple authorship criteria for canonicity problematized?

options.text: Many books have unknown or complex authorship and long development
No biblical book has an author
Only Greek books can be canonical

questions.explanation: The lecture notes that books like Judges, Kings, and Jeremiah complicate simplistic prophetic-authorship criteria.

questions.question: What does Ben Sira grandson prologue help show?

options.text: A repeated reference to the law, prophets, and other books
A fixed New Testament table of contents
That Joshua was written in Latin

questions.explanation: The prologue repeatedly mentions the law, prophets, and the others, suggesting tripartite canonical awareness.

questions.question: What does Qumran text 4QMMT possibly indicate?

options.text: That Esther was the only Scripture
A reference to Moses, the prophets, and David as authoritative categories

questions.explanation: The lecture notes 4QMMT language about the book of Moses, prophets, and David as possible evidence for canonical grouping.

questions.question: Which New Testament texts are used as canon evidence?
questions.explanation: Luke 24 mentions Law, Prophets, and Psalms, while Luke 11 may reflect a sequence from Abel to Zechariah.

questions.question: How does the lecture treat the Council of Jamnia theory?

options.text: As debunked due to lack of evidence for a formal canon-fixing event
As the decisive proof of the canon
As the origin of the Septuagint

questions.explanation: The lecture rejects Jamnia as a formal canon-fixing council with a definitive list.

questions.question: What textual transmission point is made near the end?

options.text: The Masoretic Text tradition is preserved in later manuscripts and BHS uses major codices such as Leningradensis
The Vulgate is the only Hebrew Bible
Qumran contains only New Testament books

questions.explanation: The lecture notes the Masoretic tradition, Codex Leningradensis, BHS, and Qumran evidence for the Old Testament corpus.

quizzes.title: W11 Quiz 2
quizzes.description: Joshua introduction, critical models, conquest tensions, and the herem problem

questions.question: Where is Joshua placed in the Hebrew Bible?
questions.explanation: Joshua belongs to the Former Prophets along with Judges, Samuel, and Kings.

questions.question: Which scholarly models for Joshua are discussed?
questions.explanation: The lecture surveys Hexateuch, Deuteronomistic History, and Enneateuch proposals.

questions.question: What critique is raised against strong source reconstructions?

options.text: The Pentateuch has clear canonical integrity and source consensus is lacking
There is no Joshua text to reconstruct

questions.explanation: The lecturer notes the Pentateuch functions as a strong canonical unit and source theories lack consensus.

questions.question: What does the book itself say about Joshua as author?

options.text: It explicitly claims Joshua wrote every verse
It is effectively anonymous and includes later shaping indicators
It names Ezra in the first verse
It denies any connection to Joshua

questions.explanation: Tradition associates Joshua with the book, but the text is anonymous and includes death accounts and to-this-day notices.

questions.question: What is the major conquest tension in Joshua?

options.text: It denies that Israel crossed the Jordan
It claims total conquest/rest while also saying much remains unconquered

questions.explanation: Joshua contains both complete-conquest summaries and admissions that land and peoples remain.

questions.question: How does Joshua overlap with Judges?

options.text: They are written in Greek only
They share settlement themes and repeated Joshua death notices

questions.explanation: The lecture notes overlap with Judges, including repeated death notices and differing impressions of settlement.

questions.question: What kind of narrative issue is raised by the wordplay on Ai?

options.text: Etiological or ideological storytelling and biblical historiography

questions.explanation: Ai as a heap of ruins is used as an example of etiological or ideological narrative questions.

questions.question: Which non-narrative materials are prominent in Joshua 13-21?

options.text: Boundary and city lists, including priestly materials

questions.explanation: The allotment section includes lists, Levitical cities, and cities of refuge.

questions.question: What ethical issue does the lecture foreground?

options.text: The herem or ban commands in the conquest narratives

questions.explanation: The lecture highlights the moral and theological difficulty of the ban language.

questions.question: Which scholarly response to conquest rhetoric is noted?

options.text: Origen denying the Old Testament exists
John Walton reading conquest language as ancient rhetorical convention or hyperbole

questions.explanation: The lecture mentions John Walton and genre-based readings such as propaganda or hyperbole.

quizzes.title: W11 Quiz 3
quizzes.description: Joshua as Moses successor, new Exodus, conquest, allotment, and incomplete possession

questions.question: How is Joshua marked as Moses true successor?

options.text: He receives Moses title servant of the LORD

questions.explanation: The shared title servant of the LORD signals Joshua legitimacy as Moses successor.

questions.question: What Deuteronomic ideal does Joshua embody?

options.text: Torah obedience without turning right or left and meditation day and night

questions.explanation: Joshua is portrayed in terms of steadfast Torah obedience, echoing Deuteronomy and Psalm 1 themes.

questions.question: What is the macro-structure of Joshua in the lecture?
questions.explanation: The lecture outlines Joshua 1-12 as conquest, 13-21 as allotment, and 22-24 as farewell material.

questions.question: What role does Rahab play in Joshua 2?

options.text: She rejects all knowledge of Yahweh
She writes the law of Moses
She exemplifies faith based on hearing Yahweh acts

questions.explanation: Rahab responds in trust after hearing of Yahweh deeds while the nations hearts melt with fear.

questions.question: How are Joshua 3-4 interpreted?

options.text: As a new Exodus crossing centered on the ark and memorial stones

questions.explanation: The Jordan crossing echoes the Red Sea, with waters standing and memorial stones preserving the event.

questions.question: What effect is the Jordan crossing meant to have among the nations?
questions.explanation: The lecture emphasizes that the event magnifies Yahweh and spreads fear of him among the nations.

questions.question: What tension follows Joshua 11 summary of rest from war?

options.text: Whether Joshua ever crossed the Jordan
Whether Moses was born in Egypt
Whether possession of the land secures covenant faithfulness

questions.explanation: Even after conquest summaries, the narrative raises whether land possession is enough for faithfulness.

questions.question: How does the allotment section complicate the idea of complete rest?

options.text: Joshua is old and much land remains

questions.explanation: Joshua 13 opens with Joshua old and much land still remaining, shifting from conquest to apportionment.

questions.question: Which tribes are cited as examples of incomplete conquest?

options.text: Only Simeon and Levi before Sinai

questions.explanation: The lecture notes remaining Jebusites, Canaanites, and the issue of iron chariots in tribal allotments.

questions.question: What problem appears in Joshua 18?

options.text: Seven tribes delay taking possession, so Joshua orders a survey and division by lot
Jericho is rebuilt before it falls
No one knows where Shiloh is

questions.explanation: Despite the land being described as subdued, seven tribes have not taken possession and must survey the land.

quizzes.title: W11 Quiz 4
quizzes.description: Joshua 21-24, fulfilled promises, remaining responsibility, covenant loyalty, and future hope

questions.question: What does Joshua 21:43-45 emphasize?

options.text: God gave all the land and not one word of his promise failed

questions.explanation: The passage stacks fulfillment language: all the land, rest, no enemy standing, and no word falling.

questions.question: How can fulfillment language coexist with remaining land?

options.text: The lecture says the text contradicts itself with no purpose
The lecture denies any remaining nations
The lecture distinguishes allotment from actual occupation and ongoing responsibility

questions.explanation: The book can affirm God fulfilled his promise while still presenting Israel responsibility to take and hold allotted land.

questions.question: What crisis occurs in Joshua 22?

options.text: An altar by the eastern tribes nearly causes civil war until explained as a memorial witness
The ark is captured by Philistines

questions.explanation: The western tribes fear apostasy, but the altar is explained as a witness rather than a rival worship site.

questions.question: What does Joshua 23 acknowledge despite rest?

options.text: Nations remain and must still be inherited

questions.explanation: Joshua gathers leaders, credits God as the one who fought, and still speaks of remaining nations.

questions.question: What command echoes Joshua 1 in Joshua 23?

options.text: Be very strong to keep the Law of Moses and do not turn right or left

questions.explanation: Joshua farewell repeats the call to strength and Torah obedience.

questions.question: What will idolatry do according to Joshua warning?

options.text: Cause God to stop driving out nations and Israel to perish from the land

questions.explanation: Joshua warns that foreign gods will reverse Israel fortunes and threaten continued possession of the land.

questions.question: Where does Joshua 24 renew covenant?
questions.explanation: Joshua 24 gathers Israel at Shechem and rehearses God acts before calling for exclusive service.

questions.question: Why does Joshua say the people cannot serve the LORD?

options.text: They still must deal with idols before a holy and jealous God
They forgot how to speak Hebrew

questions.explanation: Joshua warns that they cannot serve a holy, jealous God while idols remain among them.

questions.question: What do Joseph bones signify at the end of Joshua?

options.text: The failure of the land promise
Arrival and settlement in continuity with Genesis 50 and Exodus 13

questions.explanation: Joseph bones function as a signpost that earlier promises about return to the land have reached a settlement marker.

questions.question: What canonical trajectory does the lecture draw from Joshua ending?

options.text: Former Prophets diagnose the heart problem; Latter Prophets hold out new-covenant Spirit hope

questions.explanation: Joshua ends with unresolved obedience concerns, pointing toward the broader Former Prophets diagnosis and later prophetic hope.

quizzes.title: W12 Quiz 1
quizzes.description: Judges authorship, dating, canonical authority, structure, and chronology

questions.question: What does Judges 11:26 suggest about the book composition?

options.text: A long time horizon, since it refers to about 300 years
That Judges was written before Moses
That the book covers only one week

questions.explanation: The 300-year reference implies significant distance from early events and supports later compilation.

questions.question: What do the no king in Israel refrains imply?

options.text: A perspective aware of monarchy or its need
That Judges is only about priests

questions.explanation: The repeated refrain suggests the author writes from a vantage point where kingship is known or desired as a remedy.

questions.question: Why is Judges 18:30 important for dating discussions?

options.text: It dates the book to the flood
It mentions captivity of the land

questions.explanation: The captivity reference may indicate later editorial updating or an exilic/captivity horizon.

questions.question: How does the lecture connect authorship and canon?

options.text: Only books signed by Moses count
Authority rests on Scripture being God-breathed, not knowing the human author

questions.explanation: The lecture emphasizes theopneustos and pasagraphe: Scripture authority does not depend on identifying every human author.

questions.question: What cycle is introduced in Judges 2?

options.text: Disobedience, divine anger, repentance or groaning, and salvation

questions.explanation: The lecture follows the recurring Judges cycle of sin, oppression, cry, and deliverance.

questions.question: Which sections function as concluding narrative units outside the main cycle?
questions.explanation: Micah/Dan and the Levite concubine narratives are treated as independent concluding units.

questions.question: What does judge often mean in Judges?

options.text: Deliverer or war leader more than courtroom adjudicator

questions.explanation: The lecture distinguishes the judges from modern legal judges, though Moses can serve as a contrast for adjudication.

questions.question: How are minor and major judges distinguished?

options.text: Minor judges receive brief notices; major judges get extended narratives

questions.explanation: The lecture notes the difference between brief mentions and fuller narratives such as Gideon.

questions.question: Why is Judges chronology difficult?

options.text: It uses many round numbers and does not align neatly with broader timelines such as 1 Kings 480 years
It has no numbers at all
It gives exact modern dates for every event

questions.explanation: The prevalence of round numbers and tension with other chronological claims suggests non-precision.

questions.question: What does the lecture infer about the compiler of Judges?

options.text: The compiler wrote only a neutral diary
The compiler selected and arranged traditions to communicate a theological message
The compiler ignored all earlier traditions

questions.explanation: Judges is presented as a curated and arranged work with a structured theological message.

quizzes.title: W12 Quiz 2
quizzes.description: Judges 1-2 as continuity with Joshua, Judah leadership, incomplete conquest, and Bokim

questions.question: How does Judges deliberately link itself to Joshua?

options.text: It opens with after the death of Joshua

questions.explanation: The opening formula echoes Joshua and signals continuity with the preceding narrative.

questions.question: What is the function of the after the death of formula?

options.text: It proves the next book is unrelated
It links major narrative blocks compositionally

questions.explanation: The lecture compares the formula to other transitions such as 2 Samuel 1:1.

questions.question: What leadership problem appears at the beginning of Judges?

options.text: Israel asks who will go up first
There are three kings at once

questions.explanation: Unlike Moses-to-Joshua succession, Judges opens with a leadership vacuum.

questions.question: Which tribe does God appoint to lead?
questions.explanation: God designates Judah to go up, framing Judah positively and foreshadowing later leadership themes.

questions.question: What complicates Judah positive portrayal?

options.text: Even Judah has incomplete conquest, with iron chariots cited

questions.explanation: Judah has real success but does not fully drive out all inhabitants.

questions.question: Why are Caleb and Othniel material mentioned?

options.text: They prove Judges is about Rome
They replace the need for Joshua
They show no connection to earlier books
Judges reuses Joshua-era material to strengthen Judah favorable framing

questions.explanation: The lecture notes reprise of Joshua 15 material, likely selected to reinforce Judah prominence.

questions.question: How is Benjamin portrayed in relation to Jerusalem?

options.text: Benjamin fails to dispossess the Jebusites, contrasting Judah success

questions.explanation: The contrast may anticipate later Benjamin/Saul and Judah/David tensions.

questions.question: What do the failures of Manasseh and Ephraim help set up?

options.text: Ongoing Canaanite presence, conflict, and idolatry

questions.explanation: The wider tribal failures establish the conditions for later trouble.

questions.question: What happens in Judges 2:1-5?

options.text: The messenger of the LORD rebukes covenant disobedience and warns of thorns and snares

questions.explanation: The messenger interprets the conquest failures theologically as covenant disobedience.

questions.question: Why is the place called Bokim?

options.text: Because the ark is hidden there

questions.explanation: Bokim is explained etiologically from the Hebrew root for weeping.

quizzes.title: W12 Quiz 3
quizzes.description: Judges 2 as theological framework, idolatry cycle, relapse, and transition to Samuel

questions.question: What does Judges 2:6-9 repeat?
questions.explanation: The repetition links Judges to Joshua and shows thematic recapitulation.

questions.question: What does the repeated Joshua death material suggest?

options.text: Judges is not arranged as strict modern chronology
The text is only a census

questions.explanation: The lecture argues the repetition is a theological and literary bridge rather than a simple chronological sequence.

questions.question: What central problem begins in Judges 2:10?

options.text: Israel has too many faithful kings
A new generation does not know the LORD or his works

questions.explanation: The new generation forgetting Yahweh becomes the root of the cycle.

questions.question: Which idolatry language becomes a refrain in Judges?

options.text: Israel did evil in the sight of the LORD and served Baal and Asherah

questions.explanation: The lecture highlights did evil in the sight of the LORD and service to Baal/Asherah as recurring language.

questions.question: How does God respond to Israel covenant unfaithfulness?

options.text: He gives or sells them into enemy hands so they cannot stand before enemies
He makes every enemy vanish forever
He appoints Pharaoh as high priest

questions.explanation: Israel defeat reverses Joshua success because of covenant disobedience.

questions.question: What happens after each judge dies?

options.text: Israel relapses, often worse than before

questions.explanation: The cycle grows bleak because deliverance does not produce lasting heart-level obedience.

questions.question: Which phrases connect Judges to Exodus and Deuteronomy language?

options.text: Blessed are the poor in spirit
In the beginning was the Word
Did not listen to my voice and turned aside quickly

questions.explanation: The lecture links Judges phrasing to covenant warnings and golden calf language.

questions.question: Why does the LORD leave remaining nations in the land?

options.text: Because the land was never promised
Because Judah asked for no land
Because Israel transgressed the covenant and the nations become part of judgment/testing

questions.explanation: The lecture reads remaining nations as a consequence of covenant breach and a testing context.

questions.question: What positive but limited theme appears with some judges?

options.text: The Spirit of the LORD comes upon them for deliverance
They all keep the law perfectly

questions.explanation: Spirit empowerment highlights the need for divinely empowered leadership even in the dark cycles.

questions.question: How does Judges transition toward Samuel?

options.text: No king in Israel, Ephraim links, and Samuel as a judge prepare for monarchy
It resolves kingship with Saul already ruling

questions.explanation: The lecture connects Judges to 1 Samuel through shared geography, themes, and Samuel role as judge.

quizzes.title: W13 Quiz 1
quizzes.description: Samuel composition, sources, canonical placement, and textual criticism

questions.question: What do the explanatory asides in Samuel indicate?

options.text: The book has no historical setting
The book is written only for priests
The book addresses later readers who need older terms or customs explained
The book was composed before all events

questions.explanation: Examples such as seer versus prophet and Tamar robe suggest a later audience.

questions.question: Why is 1 Samuel 27:6 important for dating Samuel?

options.text: It refers to kings of Judah, implying a post-division perspective

questions.explanation: The phrase kings of Judah suggests the narrator looks from after the kingdom divided.

questions.question: Why can Samuel not be the sole author of Samuel?

options.text: Samuel dies in 1 Samuel 25 while the narrative continues
The book says David wrote every verse

questions.explanation: The narrative continues long after Samuel death, so he cannot be sole author.

questions.question: Which sources or source-like materials are mentioned?

options.text: Book of Jashar, poetic units, and records associated with Samuel, Nathan, and Gad

questions.explanation: The lecture cites the Book of Jashar, embedded poems, and 1 Chronicles 29:29.

questions.question: How did Samuel-Kings circulate in Hebrew tradition according to the lecture?

options.text: As one continuous story later divided in the Septuagint into Kingdoms
As a single verse in Deuteronomy

questions.explanation: Eusebius and Jerome are noted for the unity of Samuel-Kings in Hebrew, with LXX divisions into Kingdoms.

questions.question: What narrative role does Samuel play canonically?
questions.explanation: Samuel functions as a bridge from judgeship and the no-king problem into royal narratives.

questions.question: Which larger compositional theory is discussed with Samuel?
questions.explanation: Samuel is discussed in relation to Martin Noth Deuteronomistic History proposal.

questions.question: Why is textual criticism especially important for Samuel?

options.text: There are no manuscripts of Samuel
Samuel has prominent textual problems such as 1 Samuel 13:1 and 14:41
Every verse is identical in all traditions

questions.explanation: The lecture highlights difficult MT readings, possible scribal error, and LXX differences.

questions.question: Why is the Septuagint especially important for Samuel?

options.text: It was written by Samuel himself
Its witnesses are earlier and may preserve readings from a different Hebrew tradition

questions.explanation: Earlier Greek witnesses and Qumran evidence make the LXX highly relevant for Samuel.

questions.question: What did Qumran discoveries change about evaluating the LXX?

options.text: They proved the LXX has no Hebrew basis
They removed the need for textual criticism
They dated Samuel to the New Testament
They showed some Greek readings may reflect an alternate Hebrew Vorlage

questions.explanation: Some Dead Sea Scrolls appear to stand behind Greek translations, especially significant for Samuel.

quizzes.title: W13 Quiz 2
quizzes.description: Samuel structure, the sha al motif, Hannah song, monarchy, and the Davidic covenant

questions.question: What reading strategy does the lecture use for Samuel?

options.text: Focus on macro-composition and theological purpose

questions.explanation: The course emphasizes Samuel overall structure and framing poems rather than verse-by-verse coverage.

questions.question: What is the proposed macro-structure of Samuel?

options.text: Samuel, Saul, David, and appendix sections

questions.explanation: The lecture outlines 1 Sam 1-12, 1 Sam 13-31, 2 Sam 1-20, and 2 Sam 21-24.

questions.question: What does the Hebrew root sha al mean and connect?

options.text: Ask/request, linking Hannah, Samuel, Saul, and the request for a king

questions.explanation: The repeated ask/request motif runs from Hannah petition to Saul and Israel request for monarchy.

questions.question: Why is Hannah song more than private thanksgiving?

options.text: It is only a legal code
It is written after exile only
It uses expansive reversal language that exceeds Hannah immediate circumstances

questions.explanation: The poem speaks of enemies, death and life, and cosmic reversal beyond Hannah personal story.

questions.question: What does Hannah song mention before any king appears?
questions.explanation: The song climaxes with language about Yahweh king and anointed, signaling the monarchy theme.

questions.question: How is Israel request for a king in 1 Samuel 8 framed?

options.text: Negatively, as wanting to be like the nations and rejecting Yahweh kingship
As a request for no ruler

questions.explanation: The request is treated as a theological rejection of Yahweh rule.

questions.question: What role does Saul serve in the narrative?

options.text: Historical king, foil to David, and embodiment of Benjamin/Judah tensions

questions.explanation: Saul is not merely a historical figure; he also functions literarily and theologically in contrast to David.

questions.question: What is the house reversal in 2 Samuel 7?

options.text: Solomon destroys the temple before building it
David will not build Yahweh a house; Yahweh will build David a dynasty

questions.explanation: Yahweh reverses David plan and promises to build David a house, meaning dynasty.

questions.question: What does the Davidic promise emphasize?

options.text: Seed, throne, kingdom, forever, and father-son relationship

questions.explanation: The lecture highlights seed, forever language, and royal sonship in 2 Samuel 7.

questions.question: How does the broader canon treat the promised Davidic son?

options.text: It says Saul fulfilled it forever
It keeps wrestling with the identity because Solomon is not ultimate fulfillment

questions.explanation: Kings can initially point to Solomon, but his failures prevent final resolution.

quizzes.title: W13 Quiz 3
quizzes.description: Samuel closing poems, messianic hope, Davidic covenant tension, and unresolved fulfillment

questions.question: How does the lecture relate 2 Samuel 22 to Hannah song?

options.text: They function as bookends around Samuel narrative
They both come from Chronicles only

questions.explanation: The lecture reads Hannah poem and David closing poem as framing the book.

questions.question: Which motifs link Hannah poem and David psalm?

options.text: Yahweh as rock, divine thunder, death-to-life deliverance, king and anointed

questions.explanation: The shared imagery supports the bookend reading and messianic direction.

questions.question: Why does the placement of 2 Samuel 22 matter?

options.text: It comes after David moral and familial failures
It comes before Hannah is born

questions.explanation: Because David has already failed grievously, the poem complicates a simple David-is-the-final-fulfillment reading.

questions.question: Why is 2 Samuel 22:51 emphasized?

options.text: Its singular king and anointed language may point beyond David to a future seed
It lists the tribes by size

questions.explanation: The singular language raises whether the referent is David or a future messianic figure.

questions.question: What interpretive issue appears in 2 Samuel 23:1?

options.text: Whether the temple exists in Genesis
Whether David speaks concerning the anointed of the God of Jacob

questions.explanation: Translation differences affect whether the verse is read as oriented toward the anointed figure.

questions.question: How does the lecture read 2 Samuel 23:5?

options.text: David claims his house has been perfectly faithful
David admits his house is not so with God yet trusts an everlasting covenant

questions.explanation: The verse is read as candid about David household failure while resting in God ordered covenant.

questions.question: What does the covenant not yet sprouting suggest?

options.text: The promise remains unrealized and awaits fulfillment

questions.explanation: The lecture treats the sprouting language as unfulfilled hope rather than full realization in David house.

questions.question: Why is Uriah appearance among the mighty men significant?

options.text: It removes Bathsheba from the story

questions.explanation: Naming Uriah at the end reminds readers of David adultery and murder.

questions.question: What resonance is noted in David purchase of the threshing floor?
questions.explanation: The lecture links 2 Samuel 24 to Abrahamic purchase language and covenantal themes.

questions.question: What conclusion does the lecture draw about eternal kingship in Samuel?

options.text: It is fully realized in Saul
It is abandoned by the canon
It belongs only to the northern kingdom
It is anticipated but not realized in David or his immediate house

questions.explanation: Samuel ends with covenant hope still awaiting a future anointed king.

quizzes.title: W14 Quiz 1
quizzes.description: Final exam format, true-false sources, and three essay preparation areas

questions.question: How does the final exam differ from the midterm format?

options.text: It has no multiple choice, only true/false and essays

questions.explanation: The instructor says the final mirrors the midterm except that there are no multiple-choice questions.

questions.question: Where will some true/false questions come from?

options.text: Textbook quizzes starting around Numbers and moving forward

questions.explanation: About 10-15 true/false items will be drawn from prior textbook quizzes from Numbers onward.

questions.question: What is the lecture-based true/false focus?

options.text: Idolatry-related material in late Exodus, Leviticus, and Deuteronomy

questions.explanation: The larger lecture pool focuses on idolatry passages after the law section in Exodus and in Leviticus and Deuteronomy.

questions.question: How many possible essay prompts must students prepare?

options.text: Three possible prompts, of which two will appear

questions.explanation: The instructor says students should prepare all three essay areas because two will be selected.

questions.question: What is the first essay area about?

options.text: Interrelated idolatry texts in the Pentateuch

questions.explanation: Essay one asks about the issue and importance of idolatry-related texts in the Pentateuch.

questions.question: Which passages are examples for the idolatry essay?

options.text: Exodus 32, Leviticus 17, Numbers 25, Deuteronomy 9, and Deuteronomy 32:16-21

questions.explanation: The review names these Pentateuchal idolatry passages as key examples.

questions.question: What special interpretive features should be included in the idolatry essay?

options.text: Intertextuality, wordplay such as atbash, jealousy, and the nations/Gentiles theme

questions.explanation: The instructor expects attention to links, wordplays, and the theological message including jealousy and nations language.

questions.question: What is the second essay area about?

options.text: Conquest or no conquest tensions in Joshua

questions.explanation: Essay two focuses on Joshua texts that affirm completed conquest while also noting remaining land.

questions.question: Which Joshua texts are emphasized for the second essay?
questions.explanation: The review highlights summary/rest passages and texts about remaining inheritance.

questions.question: What is the third essay area about?

options.text: Judges authorship indicators and critical issues

questions.explanation: Essay three covers Judges authorship clues and critical issues such as independent units, the role of judge, and chronology.

quizzes.title: W14 Quiz 2
quizzes.description: Kings introduction, composition, sources, purpose, prophetic evaluation, and arrangement

questions.question: Why can Kings not be a single eyewitness chronicle?

options.text: It spans from David/Solomon succession to Jehoiachin release centuries later

questions.explanation: The narrative runs from about 970 BC to about 560 BC, requiring a later compiler.

questions.question: What does the absence of Cyrus edict suggest?

options.text: That Kings was written after the New Testament
That Cyrus appears in every chapter
A late-exilic, likely pre-539 composition or a different restoration framing

questions.explanation: Since Kings ends before the return under Cyrus, the lecture suggests a late-exilic setting, likely before 539/540 BC.

questions.question: What is true about the author of Kings?

options.text: The book is anonymous but authoritative
The book names Solomon as sole author
The book is signed by Elijah
The book has no canonical status

questions.explanation: As with Judges, anonymous authorship does not remove canonical authority.

questions.question: Which sources does Kings cite?

options.text: Chronicles of the kings and the Book of the Acts of Solomon

questions.explanation: Kings repeatedly directs readers to source materials such as royal chronicles and acts of Solomon.

questions.question: What do until this day notes indicate?

options.text: That the author lived before Moses
That all events happened in one day
Temporal distance and/or incorporation of older source material

questions.explanation: Such notices point to a later viewpoint or preserved source layers.

questions.question: How does Kings relate to Judges and Samuel?

options.text: It replaces them with unrelated wisdom sayings
It belongs only to the Torah
It continues their themes and narrative flow into monarchy

questions.explanation: Kings continues the succession narrative and monarchy themes prepared by Judges and Samuel.

questions.question: How does the lecture describe Kings historical presentation?
questions.explanation: Kings points elsewhere for fuller detail and selects episodes for theological evaluation.

questions.question: Why are Elijah and Elisha central?

options.text: Prophets function as covenant enforcers and evaluators of kings

questions.explanation: Prophetic narratives receive attention because prophets interpret and enforce covenant standards.

questions.question: What does the brief treatment of Omri show?

options.text: The author prioritizes theological purpose over exhaustive political prominence

questions.explanation: Omri was significant externally but receives brief coverage because Kings selects material theologically.

questions.question: How does the lecture treat chronological irregularities in 2 Kings 18-20?

options.text: As proof the book has no purpose
As a command to ignore the passage
As a late New Testament insertion
As deliberate arrangement or recapitulation serving the message

questions.explanation: The lecture encourages reading non-linear sequencing as message-serving structure.

quizzes.title: W14 Quiz 3
quizzes.description: Kings purpose, Davidic promise, judgment, Israel and Judah, chronology, and hope

questions.question: What major question does Kings ask about the Davidic promise?

options.text: Whether Solomon or any Davidic son fulfills 2 Samuel 7

questions.explanation: The book tests whether David descendants fulfill the promised enduring throne.

questions.question: What second major purpose does Kings serve?

options.text: Explaining Israel and Judah judgment as covenant unfaithfulness

questions.explanation: Kings gives the theological rationale for national defeat and exile.

questions.question: What two paradigms are kings measured against?

options.text: David wholehearted loyalty and Jeroboam sin

questions.explanation: David is the positive model; Jeroboam golden calf cult becomes the negative model.

questions.question: How is the northern kingdom characterized?
questions.explanation: All northern rulers are evaluated negatively for perpetuating Jeroboam sin, with frequent dynastic turnover.

questions.question: How does Judah differ from Israel politically?

options.text: Judah lasts longer with Davidic dynastic continuity, except Athaliah interruption

questions.explanation: The Davidic line gives Judah more continuity, though Judah still fails morally and spiritually.

questions.question: What point from Michael Kugler is highlighted?

options.text: The Davidic covenant cancels every law
Sinai covenant obligations remain primary, so the Davidic covenant is not a shield from punishment

questions.explanation: The lecture says the Davidic promise cannot be read simplistically as unconditional security against judgment.

questions.question: What sins explain exile in Kings?
questions.explanation: Kings explains judgment primarily in covenantal terms: idolatry, injustice, and disobedience.

questions.question: Which chronological issues complicate Kings?

options.text: Accession-year counting, co-regencies, calendars, and textual variants

questions.explanation: The lecture notes several technical reasons regnal chronology is difficult.

questions.question: What is the three-part outline of Kings?

options.text: Solomon; divided monarchy to Israel fall; Judah to Jerusalem fall

questions.explanation: The lecture outlines 1 Kings 1-11, 1 Kings 12-2 Kings 17, and 2 Kings 18-25.

questions.question: What note of hope appears at the end of Kings?

options.text: The temple is rebuilt by Solomon again
A Davidic king is honored in Babylon

questions.explanation: Jehoiachin elevation in Babylon suggests the Davidic line is not extinguished.

quizzes.title: W14 Quiz 4
quizzes.description: Solomon succession, conditional Davidic promise, temple dedication, prayer, and warning

questions.question: How does 1 Kings 1-2 portray Solomon succession?

options.text: Solomon is legitimate through Bathsheba and the prophet while David appears weak

questions.explanation: The opening shows David frailty and political vulnerability while establishing Solomon legitimacy.

questions.question: What central theological question does Kings press?

options.text: Who is the true Davidic king who fulfills 2 Samuel 7?

questions.explanation: The succession narrative introduces the unresolved search for the promised Davidic king.

questions.question: What is the focus of David charge to Solomon?

options.text: Obedience to the Mosaic law, echoing Joshua 1

questions.explanation: David charges Solomon to keep statutes, commands, judgments, and testimonies in Mosaic-law language.

questions.question: How is the Davidic promise increasingly framed in Kings?

options.text: With an if condition tied to obedience

questions.explanation: Kings repeatedly connects dynastic continuity to wholehearted obedience.

questions.question: Which early Solomon details foreshadow trouble?

options.text: His exile to Babylon before becoming king
Marriage to Pharaoh daughter and spending longer on his own house than the temple

questions.explanation: The lecture sees foreign marriage and building priorities as early warning signs.

questions.question: Why does 1 Kings 8 appear climactic?

options.text: The glory fills the temple and Solomon uses promise-fulfillment language

questions.explanation: The dedication looks like a high point as God presence fills the temple and Solomon celebrates fulfilled words.

questions.question: What does Solomon prayer stress about God dwelling?

options.text: God cannot be contained in the temple and must hear from heaven
God is trapped inside the temple
God cannot hear prayer after Solomon

questions.explanation: The repeated hear from heaven language emphasizes God transcendence beyond the temple.

questions.question: What future crises does Solomon prayer anticipate?

options.text: Sin, covenant curses, exile, and restoration upon return

questions.explanation: The prayer echoes Deuteronomy 28 and 30 with curses, exile, repentance, forgiveness, and return.

questions.question: Why does Solomon pray for foreigners?

options.text: So the temple can exclude all nations
So all nations may know and fear the LORD
So foreigners can replace all covenant promises

questions.explanation: The prayer includes an international dimension centered on the LORD name.

questions.question: What does God warning in 1 Kings 9 say?

options.text: The temple prevents all exile automatically
Obedience brings establishment, but idolatry brings land cutoff and temple rejection

questions.explanation: God response makes the future explicitly conditional on obedience versus idolatry.

quizzes.title: W14 Quiz 5
quizzes.description: Solomon decline, Deuteronomy 17 violations, exile, Jehoiachin hope, and Isaiah transition

questions.question: How does the lecture describe 1 Kings 10 for Solomon?

options.text: The start of the exile under Cyrus

questions.explanation: Despite prosperity, 1 Kings 10 is read as the start of Solomon unraveling.

questions.question: What Deuteronomy 17 warning does Solomon wealth violate?

options.text: The king must never read the law
The king must not multiply wealth
The king must destroy the temple
The king must live in Egypt

questions.explanation: Gold, silver, and precious goods show Solomon multiplying wealth against the royal law.

questions.question: Why are Solomon horses from Egypt significant?

options.text: Egyptian horses were required by the law
They prove Solomon was a priest
Deuteronomy 17 forbids multiplying horses and returning to Egypt

questions.explanation: The lecture sees horse importation from Egypt as a direct Deuteronomic violation.

questions.question: What happens because of Solomon foreign wives?

options.text: He becomes more obedient than David
His heart turns away to other gods

questions.explanation: 1 Kings 11 states the precise result Deuteronomy warned about: Solomon heart turns away.

questions.question: What conclusion does the lecture draw about Solomon?

options.text: He is not the faithful Davidic ruler hoped for
He fully resolves the Davidic promise

questions.explanation: Solomon systematic disobedience shows he is not the final righteous Davidic king.

questions.question: What is the Deuteronomic baseball metaphor about?

options.text: A literal sport played in Jerusalem
Repeated royal failures as each king comes to the plate and strikes out

questions.explanation: The metaphor summarizes the repetitive failure pattern among the kings.

questions.question: What is Kings primary explanation for Israel defeat and exile?

options.text: Idolatry that provoked the LORD to anger

questions.explanation: The lecture highlights idolatry and provoking the LORD to anger, especially in 2 Kings 17 language.

questions.question: What does Jerusalem fall appear to threaten?

options.text: Visible covenant hopes tied to land, temple, law, and Davidic kingship

questions.explanation: The destruction looks catastrophic for the visible signs of covenant hope.

questions.question: What is the glimmer of hope in 2 Kings 25?

options.text: Jehoiachin is released and honored in Babylon

questions.explanation: Jehoiachin elevation suggests the Davidic line continues despite exile.

questions.question: In the Hebrew canonical order, where does the lecture look next for Davidic hope?
questions.explanation: The lecture notes that Isaiah follows Kings in the Hebrew order and continues the search for the Davidic king.
